APPLICATION DETAILS.Plain English Summary:
Why we are doing this research
MRI scanners are machines that use strong magnets and a type of energy (radiofrequency) to produce detailed
pictures of the inside of the body. MRI is regularly used in healthcare. In the UK, 4.1 million MRI scans were taken
from March 2022 to March 2023. As a diagnostic tool, MRI is invaluable. Yet, it comes with risks if the correct
information is not given to the radiographers before the scan. The magnetic force of the machine can cause metal
objects to move, sometimes quickly and dangerously causing harm. The magnetic force can also affect metal
objects in the body, causing them to move or twist. This could injure a patient or damage the object, stopping it
from working. The metal objects could be pacemakers, joint replacements (hip/knee) or metal pieces from old
wounds. Burns can be caused by metal objects heating inside or outside of the body.
For the safety of patients, radiographers should carefully screen every person who enters the MRI room. A patient
may not have read or understood the information given to them. They may have forgotten their surgical history or
be unconscious, leaving the potential for harm. Training for screening patients about to receive an MRI is not
compulsory but should be addressed to stop any harm to patients. Also, there are few resources to teach patients
what they should know about MRI screening. MRI scanning is safe but every person who enters the scanning
room must understand the risk and what can be done to deal with any potential problem.
Aim
To increase patient and staff knowledge, and improve MRI safety. Online (eLearning) training for staff and an
infographic and animation for patients will be made. Both will be available throughout the UK.
Our plans
The eLearning training will teach radiographers special screening methods to find and name medical devices on
X-rays. In the UK, X-rays can be used for screening unconscious patients, but currently, there is no training for
this. Also, if a patient already has X-rays, we could help patients with memory problems or communication
difficulties. This research will test the program and find out if it changes how radiographers work.
To teach patients about MRI screening, an information resource known as an infographic will be developed. It will
explain what patients need to bring with them and the information patients need to tell the radiographer.
Infographics are used in healthcare because they explain complicated information in an easy, visually inclusive
manner. A web link will be added to an animation with subtitles. This allows anyone to listen to the same
information. The infographic will be designed to be sent with appointment letters from any NHS hospital. The
animations will be made available to all NHS Trusts who wants to use it on their website. Patients will help to
design the infographic and animations.
Patient and Public Involvement (PPI)
Patients were asked their views on MRI screening. The feedback was talked about with the project’s PPI team.
They suggested using an infographic, with animations and subtitles, to help patients understand MRI safety
screening. The PPI team helped shape all parts of this research plan from the design to finding people to take part,
how to share the results and the plain English summary.
Sharing the findings
I will share the eLearning training and infographic/animation through MRI professional groups, social media,
charities, and patient forums. I will do this with the co-creation, PPI and healthcare professional groups. Also, I will
share my findings at patient events, presentations at MRI and radiology-specific conferences and articles in
scientific journals.
APPLICATION DETAILS.Scientific Abstract:
Background
Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) is increasingly utilised in healthcare, combining magnets and radiofrequency
energy to produce cross-sectional images. However, these elements can pose significant risks to patients,
potentially causing serious injury or death. Metallic objects can be turned into projectiles or medical implants inside
the body can move/twist when exposed to the magnetic field. Metallic objects in and outside of the body can heat,
causing burns. Consequently, rigorous screening is required to identify any MRI contraindications. Current
screening methods (questionnaire and verbal review) can be ineffective, especially for patients with memory
issues or communication difficulties. Radiographs can identify MRI contraindications, but no training exists to teach
radiographers how to identify medical implants on imaging, potentially resulting in implants being
missed/misidentified. Clinical consequences could include:
 Cancelled/delayed scans;
 Misidentified implants being wrongly introduced into MRI.
Furthermore, patients often do not read pre-scan information and there are few easily accessible resources to
inform patients about MRI screening and the required documentation.
Question
Can bespoke educational resources improve radiographer and patient knowledge of safety-critical information
before an MRI scan?
Aims
 To introduce an MRI training programme for radiographers, and assess its effectiveness on their
knowledge, skills and confidence to make pre-MRI safety-critical decisions;
 To review and develop patient information to increase patient knowledge and assist radiographers during
pre-MRI screening, reducing harm and improving safety.
Objectives
 Undertake a systematic review, identifying the barriers and enablers to eLearning implementation;
 Build an eLearning training program, teaching radiographers to identify implants using radiographs;
 Evaluate the changes in knowledge, skills and confidence of MRI radiographers pre- and post-training;
 Explore the impact of the training on MRI radiographers' awareness of their role in improving safety and
reducing harm to patients;
 Develop complementary patient information to improve understanding of MRI screening.
Methods
The eLearning training will be built using the UK’s Medical Research Council’s framework for complex
interventions. The evaluation will use a longitudinal concurrent mixed-methods approach, to understand the
program’s impact on radiographers, their professional practice and observed changes in patient safety.
A patient infographic and animation will be designed by co-creation utilising participatory action research.
Delivery timelines
Year 1: Ethics applications, the training build and systematic review will be completed;
Year 2: The training evaluation and infographic/animation development will start;
Year 3: Writing and submitting PhD thesis.
Before each objective commences, the required personal training will be completed.
Impact
Radiographers:
 Increased knowledge and confidence in safety-critical decision-making;
 Greater awareness of medical implants;
 Greater utility of existing MRI safety policies;
 Improving safety.
Patients:
 Allows time to recall their medical history;
 Faster access to MRI, especially in emergencies;
 Less risk of injury.
NHS:
 Fewer delayed/cancelled appointments, saving money and reducing waiting lists;
 Free training, reducing pressure on training budgets.
Dissemination
The eLearning training and infographic will be promoted through MRI professional organisations (e.g., the British
Association of MR Radiographers), social media, charities and patient forums assisted by my co-creation, PPI and
steering groups. Each aspect of the research will be published in scientific journals and presented at radiology-
specific conferences.

Statement of support
We are delighted to support Hannah’s application for an NIHR Doctoral Clinical and Practitioner Academic
Fellowship (DCAF). Hannah is currently a Senior Magnetic Resonance Imaging Radiographer at The Royal
Marsden NHS Foundation Trust (RM) and the fellowship will be hosted in the RM radiotherapy department in
partnership with The Institute of Cancer Research (ICR) in the Division of Radiotherapy and Imaging. Both
organisations have a long-standing relationship through the NIHR RM/ICR Biomedical Research Centre (BRC)
and are experienced in supporting clinicians such as Hannah to practise during their fellowships, combining
research roles with clinical duties. The supervisory team is led by Professor Helen McNair, a highly experienced
supervisor with 5 completions of MD/PhD and over 14 MSc students. Hannah’s supervisors and wider clinical
team have proudly supported Hannah’s development during her NIHR PCAF and are committed to develop
aspiring clinical academic radiographers during, and after doctoral studies.
The Radiography Department:
Radiography at RM is a recognised centre of clinical excellence combined with successful academic research.
Professor Helen McNair, Hannah’s primary supervisor is herself a HEE/NIHR Senior Clinical Lecturer (2019-2024),
our Trust Lead Research Radiographer. She is Chair of the NIHR Diagnostic and therapeutic Incubator, a
collaboration with Imperial College Academic Health Science Centre. Professor McNair has established a vibrant
community of research-interested and -active radiographers which strives to increase research capacity and
capability across all radiographers. She will be able to play a key role in Hannah’s development as a clinical
academic.
Hannah has been motivated by this positive and supportive research environment to find solutions to a clinical
issue she is particularly interested in, MRI safety. Hannah’s specific clinical expertise in this area, her previous
work in building networks nationally and internationally, and her work on the PCAF makes her an excellent
candidate for an NIHR DCAF. We have no doubt that she will use this fellowship to improve the safe care for all
patients receiving MRIs. The supervisory team includes Dr Christina Malamateniou, Associate Professor in
radiography at City University of London and research lead of the department of radiography. She brings
extensive expertise in Hannahs area of interest, MRI safety but also experience of supervision, using mixed
methods and AI. Hannah will have access to the innovative training Christina offers in AI and other emerging
areas in radiography at City.
The NIHR RM/ICR Biomedical Research Centre (BRC):
The NIHR RM/ICR BRC is the only BRC specifically focused on cancer. The research strategy seeks to inspire
the future generation of researchers and to ensure equality, diversity and inclusion is visible to all. Hannah’s work
aligns with the Imaging and Data Science theme of our BRC as MRI is increasingly used in the cancer diagnosis,
treatment, and staging pathway. The BRC's main priority is to support translational research, and the work Hannah
proposes aligns very strongly with these intentions.
The Institute of Cancer Research
Hannah will be part of the Division of Radiotherapy and Imaging of the ICR, led by Professor Kevin Harrington.
The division includes the prestigious Centre for Cancer Imaging and has long-standing clinical imaging research
programmes in computed tomography (CT) and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). The ICR holds the European
Commission HR Excellence in Research Award and is a signatory of the revised Concordat to support the career
development of researchers and Concordat to support research integrity. ICR has a strong academic supportive
environment and research community, over the 2021/22 academic year, there were 264 people registered on
research degrees with the ICR including 41 new fully funded PhD students. Of ICR non-clinical PhD students
graduating between 2010 and 2019, 91% went directly into research roles.
Training and development for Hannah include courses related to research skills, statistics and scientific computing
and careers and leadership activities that are specifically tailored to researchers at Hannah’s stage, but this will be
complimented with links the supervisors have to professional specific groups and opportunities.
The Institute of Cancer research (ICR), and the RM place equality, diversity, and inclusion (EDI) at the forefront of
their joint research strategy, hosting regular events to promote its importance and monitor actions. Both
organisations attract staff and students from across the world from completely different backgrounds with a variety
of religious, sexual and gender identities. We are proud of this incredible diversity.
The Royal Marsden NHS Foundation Trust:
The RM prides itself on its track record for developing clinical academic careers for Nurses, Allied Health
Professionals and Pharmacists (NAHPPs) and is fully supportive of Hannah’s application. She is already part of
our community of researchers pursuing a clinical academic pathway including staff on internships [n= 1 HEE/NIHR
programme; n=4 internally], pre-doctoral fellowship [n=9 internally funded; n=4 NIHR PCAF] and doctoral
programmes [n=11, 2 on NIHR DCAF] and NIHR SCPRA awards (n=2). All staff on these programmes are
expected to participate in a monthly research meeting. Hannah gets an opportunity to share her work with the
group and learn from staff who are further along the pathway i.e. staff doing an NIHR doctoral fellowship or who
can share their experiences of patient and public engagement as early-career researchers. In addition, we have
20 NAHPPs who are post-doctoral and act as role models throughout the Trust.
Professor Susanne Cruickshank, the Strategic Lead for Applied Health Research, is one of 15 Research Leads in
RM. She has overall responsibility for the clinical academic programmes for NAHPPs within the Trust. As an
experienced researcher, cancer expert and educator she provides leadership and guidance so staff can flourish as
researchers and will continue to act as one of Hannah’s supervisors. She has 6 PhD and over 20 MRES/MSc
completions across different disciplines and brings a breadth of research methodological experience.
Hannah would continue to be supported beyond the completion of this doctoral fellowship to enable her to develop
her future clinical academic career by our organisations. Overall, this collaborative support from The Royal
Marsden, ICR and her supervisory team represents an unparalleled training opportunity for Hannah and
foundation for a leadership role in research.

plain_english_summary.text:
MRI scanners are machines that use strong magnets and a type of energy (radiofrequency) to produce detailed
pictures of the inside of the body. MRI is regularly used in healthcare. In the UK, 4.1 million MRI scans were taken
from March 2022 to March 2023. As a diagnostic tool, MRI is invaluable. Yet, it comes with risks if the correct
information is not given to the radiographers before the scan. The magnetic force of the machine can cause metal
objects to move, sometimes quickly and dangerously causing harm. The magnetic force can also affect metal
objects in the body, causing them to move or twist. This could injure a patient or damage the object, stopping it
from working. The metal objects could be pacemakers, joint replacements (hip/knee) or metal pieces from old
wounds. Burns can be caused by metal objects heating inside or outside of the body.
For the safety of patients, radiographers should carefully screen every person who enters the MRI room. A patient
may not have read or understood the information given to them. They may have forgotten their surgical history or
be unconscious, leaving the potential for harm. Training for screening patients about to receive an MRI is not
compulsory but should be addressed to stop any harm to patients. Also, there are few resources to teach patients
what they should know about MRI screening. MRI scanning is safe but every person who enters the scanning
room must understand the risk and what can be done to deal with any potential problem.
Aim
To increase patient and staff knowledge, and improve MRI safety. Online (eLearning) training for staff and an
infographic and animation for patients will be made. Both will be available throughout the UK.
Our plans
The eLearning training will teach radiographers special screening methods to find and name medical devices on
X-rays. In the UK, X-rays can be used for screening unconscious patients, but currently, there is no training for
this. Also, if a patient already has X-rays, we could help patients with memory problems or communication
difficulties. This research will test the program and find out if it changes how radiographers work.
To teach patients about MRI screening, an information resource known as an infographic will be developed. It will
explain what patients need to bring with them and the information patients need to tell the radiographer.
Infographics are used in healthcare because they explain complicated information in an easy, visually inclusive
manner. A web link will be added to an animation with subtitles. This allows anyone to listen to the same
information. The infographic will be designed to be sent with appointment letters from any NHS hospital. The
animations will be made available to all NHS Trusts who wants to use it on their website. Patients will help to
design the infographic and animations.
Patient and Public Involvement (PPI)
Patients were asked their views on MRI screening. The feedback was talked about with the project’s PPI team.
They suggested using an infographic, with animations and subtitles, to help patients understand MRI safety
screening. The PPI team helped shape all parts of this research plan from the design to finding people to take part,
how to share the results and the plain English summary.
Sharing the findings
I will share the eLearning training and infographic/animation through MRI professional groups, social media,
charities, and patient forums. I will do this with the co-creation, PPI and healthcare professional groups. Also, I will
share my findings at patient events, presentations at MRI and radiology-specific conferences and articles in
scientific journals.

scientific_abstract.text:
Background
Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) is increasingly utilised in healthcare, combining magnets and radiofrequency
energy to produce cross-sectional images. However, these elements can pose significant risks to patients,
potentially causing serious injury or death. Metallic objects can be turned into projectiles or medical implants inside
the body can move/twist when exposed to the magnetic field. Metallic objects in and outside of the body can heat,
causing burns. Consequently, rigorous screening is required to identify any MRI contraindications. Current
screening methods (questionnaire and verbal review) can be ineffective, especially for patients with memory
issues or communication difficulties. Radiographs can identify MRI contraindications, but no training exists to teach
radiographers how to identify medical implants on imaging, potentially resulting in implants being
missed/misidentified. Clinical consequences could include:
 Cancelled/delayed scans;
 Misidentified implants being wrongly introduced into MRI.
Furthermore, patients often do not read pre-scan information and there are few easily accessible resources to
inform patients about MRI screening and the required documentation.
Question
Can bespoke educational resources improve radiographer and patient knowledge of safety-critical information
before an MRI scan?
Aims
 To introduce an MRI training programme for radiographers, and assess its effectiveness on their
knowledge, skills and confidence to make pre-MRI safety-critical decisions;
 To review and develop patient information to increase patient knowledge and assist radiographers during
pre-MRI screening, reducing harm and improving safety.
Objectives
 Undertake a systematic review, identifying the barriers and enablers to eLearning implementation;
 Build an eLearning training program, teaching radiographers to identify implants using radiographs;
 Evaluate the changes in knowledge, skills and confidence of MRI radiographers pre- and post-training;
 Explore the impact of the training on MRI radiographers' awareness of their role in improving safety and
reducing harm to patients;
 Develop complementary patient information to improve understanding of MRI screening.
Methods
The eLearning training will be built using the UK’s Medical Research Council’s framework for complex
interventions. The evaluation will use a longitudinal concurrent mixed-methods approach, to understand the
program’s impact on radiographers, their professional practice and observed changes in patient safety.
A patient infographic and animation will be designed by co-creation utilising participatory action research.
Delivery timelines
Year 1: Ethics applications, the training build and systematic review will be completed;
Year 2: The training evaluation and infographic/animation development will start;
Year 3: Writing and submitting PhD thesis.
Before each objective commences, the required personal training will be completed.
Impact
Radiographers:
 Increased knowledge and confidence in safety-critical decision-making;
 Greater awareness of medical implants;
 Greater utility of existing MRI safety policies;
 Improving safety.
Patients:
 Allows time to recall their medical history;
 Faster access to MRI, especially in emergencies;
 Less risk of injury.
NHS:
 Fewer delayed/cancelled appointments, saving money and reducing waiting lists;
 Free training, reducing pressure on training budgets.
Dissemination
The eLearning training and infographic will be promoted through MRI professional organisations (e.g., the British
Association of MR Radiographers), social media, charities and patient forums assisted by my co-creation, PPI and
steering groups. Each aspect of the research will be published in scientific journals and presented at radiology-
specific conferences.

overall_plan.text:
This program has been developed using the Vitae researcher development
PhD program
The Institute of Cancer Research (ICR) has a long-standing clinical imaging research programme, including MRI,
and provides training in the skills necessary to excel in research. These range from interpersonal skills and
leadership to performing scientific research and project/resource management. There is an extensive catalogue to
supplement my learning, some of which are included below. Additionally, I can undertake modules from
Perspectives in Oncology. These provide a basic grounding in cancer science including medical physics, structural
biology, and cancer treatment, complementing my clinical career.
Research methods
I completed an introductory course in qualitative research methods and analysis, including content analysis.
However, further learning to report qualitative research will assist this project:
 Reporting qualitative data, NatCen.
Additionally, as I have no experience in mixed-methods research, I need to complete the following:
 Mixed-methods in health research, University of Oxford.
I lack experience in evaluating complex interventions and statistics. I'll complete the following to help with
intervention evaluation:
 Introduction to Statistics and Critical Appraisal/Further Statistics for Health Science Researchers,
University of Sheffield;
 Applied Principles and Practice of Behaviour Change, University College London;
 Developing and Evaluating Complex Interventions, NCRM;
 Intervention design and evaluation, University of Sheffield.
I'll take the following to learn how to visualise data effectively and design infographics, promoting more effective
communication between the co-creation and professional graphics teams:
 Design and make Infographics, Coursera.
This project requires both co-design and co-creation work. Therefore, I will complete the following:
 Experience-based co-design and Facilitation skills course, The Point of Care Foundation;
 Co-design for All: Doing co-design in practice, Coursera.
Completing the experience-based co-design course allows participation in the Point of Care Foundation’s
community of practice, an online space to reflect, share ideas and learn from others working in co-design.
I'll attend a Participatory Action Research (PAR) course tailored to those with basic knowledge of qualitative
research methods. It covers ethical considerations, establishing rapport with participants, and involving
communities in PAR projects:
 Introduction to PAR, SRA.
To enhance my rapport with patients and research participants, I'll attend the following to complement courses
completed during the PCAF:
 The Inclusive Communication Course, Course Beetle
 Dealing with difficult situations through forum theatre, NIHR.
I have completed a scoping review, but to perform a systematic review, I need to learn additional skills e.g., quality
assessments and meta-analysis:
 Conducting a Systematic Review: A Practical Guide, Cardiff University.
To assist the dissemination and wider impact of my research, I will complete:
 Creating prize-winning scientific posters;
 Presenting research at a conference, ICR.
Complementary courses
I will complete these ICR courses in year one to ensure a strong start to the project:
 Project management for PhDs;
 Research integrity;
 Managing your research project;
 Maintaining momentum during a research project;
 Academic writing Part 1&2.
I will then complete the following to build on this foundation knowledge and strengthen my understanding of
governance, ethics and project management:
 Project Management and Research Governance short course, University of Birmingham.
Leadership and Education
I will complete these courses to develop my skills as a future research leader and educator, specifically the
development of learning resources, understanding educational theories, the appropriate use of different media and
assessing educational impact:
 Managing successful blended teams/Exploring the imposter syndrome for clinical researchers, ICR;
 Emerging clinical leaders programme, The King’s Fund;
 Educational media and technologies, Cardiff University;
 Assessing learning, Cardiff University.
Additionally, The Royal Marsden (RMH) is partnered with the Imperial College Academic Health Science Centre,
including the Clinical Academic Training Office, where I can access free training, workshops and events to support
my learning and networking throughout the award.
Continuing Professional Development
To maintain and develop my clinical and MRI safety-specific knowledge I will attend the following:
 The MRI Safety Officer course teaches MRI staff how to mitigate risks when scanning patients with
unknown medical implants and foreign bodies in situ.
 MRI safety and innovation conference, brings together MRI safety experts from around the world to
discuss the latest advancements, issues, and innovations in MRI safety.
 UK Imaging and Oncology (UKIO) is an annual, multidisciplinary conference covering all aspects of
diagnostic imaging and radiation oncology, radiology informatics, and service delivery. Attendance will be
to enhance my clinical knowledge, participate in research hubs (especially in year two where I hope to
recruit radiographers for my intervention evaluation), and present my work, if appropriate.
 The Introduction to Artificial Intelligence for radiographers course will introduce how AI is applied in
radiography contexts. It explores its strengths/weaknesses, and its impact on practice, workflows and the
patient pathway. Completion will aid in planning my proposed post-doctoral work, City University.
Clinical skills
My primary clinical role will remain at RMH, a specialist oncology and tertiary referral centre, that exposes me to
patients with complex medical histories and MRI screening challenges. With support from managers and
supervisors within the Trust, I’ll position myself as an MRI screening specialist, tailoring teaching and training to
colleagues and the wider MRI team. Additionally, RMH is building capacity for clinical academic radiographers and
applied health research. With guidance and support from my academic/practice supervisors and managers, I aim
to transition into a clinical academic role upon completing the award.
I’ll continue participating in radiology’s journal clubs, and Southwest London Imaging Academy training. I’ll
participate in RMH’s monthly research and audit forums and attend the Trust’s annual research day to foster and
maintain research networks within my organisation.
To complement my experience, I'll organise secondments to settings that face screening challenges, provide
scanning of complex implants and areas where I have no clinical experience:
 Mobile MRI/Community Diagnostic Centres where infrastructure for viewing medical records may be
limited, and support from radiologists/medical physicists may be reduced;
 A specialist cardiac centre, where I can familiarise myself with cardiac MRI, one of the UK’s largest
expansions of all imaging modalities, particularly for imaging patients with non-conditional (untested in
MRI) pacemakers;
 7Tesla (Ultra-high field strength) MRI where independent safety testing of implants/devices is crucial due
to the higher field strength not typically encountered in clinical practice.
Primary Doctoral Supervisor
PRIMARY DOCTORAL SUPERVISOR 1
Name Professor Helen McNair
Professor of Translational Therapeutic Radiographer and Lead Clinical
Institution
Academic Radiographer
Position Royal Marsden Hospital and Institute for Cancer Research
Description of support provided
Professor McNair is fully committed to supervising Hannah to complete a robust PhD. She has >100 publications
and is a frequent invited speaker, not only regarding her research areas but also in developing clinical academic
roles. She has supervised 14 MSc students, 2 MD’s and 3 PhD's. She is currently supervising one PhD, one pre-
application NIHR award and one professional doctorate project. She commits to meeting with Hannah once a
fortnight.
Previously a holder of a HEEE/NIHR senior clinical lectureship, currently mentoring two NIHR doctoral candidates
and PI of a NIHR incubator for diagnostic and therapeutic radiographers, she will be able to apply her extensive
experience in creating clinical academic pathways to advise Hannah.
Her research interests include developing training, assessing competency, and implementing new workflows. As a
therapeutic radiographer working on a MR-Linac she is acutely aware of the MRI safety screening issues. Via
training in her NIHR grant she has improved her skills in qualitative research to enable her to co-supervise all
aspects of the research. She has supervised Hannah's predoctoral work and looks forward to continuing to be part
of Hannah’s journey in becoming a clinical academic and effective researcher.
As a ICR team leader, Helen will use her links to ensure Hannah has access to relevant facilities and training. She
works closely with the clinical academic training office at Imperial College , liaises with the Biomedical Research
Centre, a partnership between RM and ICR and works alongside Professor Susanne Cruickshank, Applied Health
Research lead at RM.
Additional Academic Supervisor(s)
ADDITIONAL ACADEMIC SUPERVISOR 1
Name Dr Christina Malamateniou
Institution City University of London
Position Reader/Associate Professor in Radiography
Description of support provided
Dr Christina Malamateniou is an Associate Professor in radiography at City University of London and the research
lead of the Department of Radiography. She is an Adjunct Professor at UCC in Ireland, Associate Editor for JMIRS
and Board member of EuSoMII. She has received multiple awards and prizes for her research in MRI and AI. Her
academic career spans 21 years, she has more than 85 published papers in peer-reviewed journals, h-index 22,
grant funding of £3.25mil from different funders as PI and Co-I, and has co-authored 6 standards/guidelines on AI,
MRI safety, and paediatric MRI. She is the current chair of research for the EFRS (2023-2025). She has led the
work on the development of the MRSO/MRSE and MRSD MRI safety standards for the EFRS in Europe (MRSO
Role Descriptor: an EQF benchmarking document (efrs.eu)) and is publishing actively in this field. She has seen 2
PhD students to completion, one of which is an NIHR doctoral fellow, examined as external examiner 8 PhDs and
currently supervises another 3 PhD students, all radiographers. There are 3 research strands in her research
outputs and departmental strategy: A) technological innovation in medical imaging, B) patient-centred care in
medical imaging and C) patient safety and patient care. This project aligns fully with the last one, but also touches
the other two areas of expertise.
ADDITIONAL ACADEMIC SUPERVISOR 2
Name Professor Susanne Cruickshank
Institution The Royal Marsden NHS Foundation Trust
Strategic Lead for Applied Health Research, Health Services
Position
Research
Description of support provided
Professor Susanne Cruickshank is the Strategic Lead for Applied Health Research at the Royal Marsden, a cancer
nurse by profession and with senior leadership experience across research, education, and practice. She has 6
1st 2nd 1st
PhD completions (n=4, Supervisor; n=2 Supervisor) and over 20 MRES/MSc (n=20 as supervisor).
Within her role she leads clinical academic research capacity and capability development among Nurses, Allied
Health Professionals and Pharmacists while working closely with the NIHR BRC/ICR to share resources. She
leads the Health Services Research Group at RM, whose portfolio of funded research from NIHR and others
focuses on improving the lives and outcomes of people affected by cancer. The work is organised around three
distinct areas: A) Systematic reviews of the evidence/theory to inform care decisions and future research; B) Using
qualitative and mixed methods to improve the experiences, unmet needs, and outcomes of cancer patients; C)
Developing, testing, and implementing complex physical, psycho-social, and emotional supportive care
interventions in healthcare settings. It is this depth and breadth of experience that will complement my supervisory
team.
Clinical Supervisor(s)
PRACTICE SUPERVISOR 1
Name Dr Ian Simcock
Institution Great Ormond Street Hospital for Children NHS Foundation Trust
Position Clinical academic radiographer
Description of support provided
Dr Ian Simcock is the lead Clinical-Academic Radiographer for GOSH Radiology and completed his NIHR funded
PhD in 2021. He developed non-invasive micro-CT for human fetal post- mortem imaging following miscarriage,
winning several awards including the NIHR Biomedical Research Centre Excellence in Impact Award 2023. He
published this work in Nature and developed a public dissemination animation video with multiple charities to
explain post-mortem choices to parents.
He has recently been awarded a £200k NIHR RfPB award to continue working in this area and is named on
£1million grants.
Dr Simcock has completed further leadership and mentorship training including the HEE LSBU post-doctoral
bridging scheme, Kings Fund - Emerging Clinical Leaders and the GOSH - Developing Leaders Programme as
well as the Society of Radiographers mentorship scheme training.
He has been elected as Chair of the Society of Radiographers Research Group, sits on the Editorial Board of
Radiography Journal, is an external advisor to the NIHR Mentoring programme at UWE, and is on the Junior
Faculty of the GOSH Biomedical Research Centre.
He has successfully mentored Hannah and multiple other radiographers through their PCAF awards, providing
guidance and support to transition through their early research career. His knowledge and awareness of clinical-
academic careers will help Hannah progress, to grow her profile and influence within the national and international
radiographic and wider research community and help her develop into a future clinical-academic leader.
PRACTICE SUPERVISOR 2
Name Dr Marco Borri
Institution King's College Hospital NHS Foundation Trust
Position Lead clinical scientist
Description of support provided
I have known Hannah for more than 6 years, and I have acted as a supervisor and mentor since her time at the
Neuroradiology Department at King's College Hospital NHS Foundation Trust. I have collaborated with Hannah on
several projects related to MRI safety, and I supported Hannah in presenting them at national (IPEM) and
international (ISMRM) leading conferences on MRI safety and to publish a scientific article in Radiography. This
also led to our ongoing collaboration with eLearning for Healthcare (NHS England).
I have been a clinical and academic supervisor, and mentor, for 12 MSc students, including Hannah. As a Clinical
Scientist and former NIHR doctoral fellow, I am in a strong position to advise and support Hannah on the wider
aspects of both her professional development and her fellowship project. An integral part of my current role is to
empower radiographers to broaden their horizons and develop their clinical role, by training them and providing
them with professional and scientific support. As a certified MR Safety Expert and member of national and
international boards on MRI Safety, I am also best placed to advise Hannah on the wider reach of her project,
making sure that her fellowship and career fit into the broader conversation on MRI Safety. I have discussed these
aspects with Hannah in preparation for her application, and I plan to meet with her for an hour on a bi-monthly
basis during the fellowship to support her in her clinical and professional development.
PRACTICE SUPERVISOR 3
Name Erica Scurr DCR (R) MSc MRSO (MBMS TM)
Institution The Royal Marsden NHS Foundation Trust
Position Cross-Site Lead MRI Superintendent Radiographer
Description of support provided
I am the MRI lead radiographer responsible for clinical MRI and research services at the Royal Marsden. I am a
clinical specialist having worked in MRI for 32 years and specialising in oncology for over 20 years. I am a
respected professional, leader and educator in this field.
Since Hannah joined the Trust, I have encouraged her to develop her work as I believe it has a pivotal role in the
education of patients and staff, highlighting the importance of safe practices in MRI. With the rapid growth in MRI
services, it is important to implement accessible learning to support our workforce in all aspects of MRI safety.
Implanted devices present a particular challenge, especially in compromised and vulnerable patients. Recognising
implants before MRI is critical to positive patient outcomes. This has been known since MRI's clinical inception, but
Hannah has had the vision and drive to formalise implant identification which will be welcomed by the MRI
community and could have national and global reach.
I am passionate about patient safety and the benefit that the e-learning module will have in supporting staff to
ensure safe, high-quality patient-centred care. The infographic will inform patients and empower them to work with
radiographic professionals, ensuring safety during their imaging pathway.
In my clinical lead role, I will be available to work with Hannah to ensure that she receives the support required to
meet the aims of this valuable project and provide mentorship as she continues to develop her leadership and
academic career.
Collaborations
The Society and College of Radiographers (SCoR) and eLearning for Healthcare (eLfH, NHS England).
My eLearning training intervention will be held in the Clinical Imaging section of the eLfH platform (https://www.e-
lfh.org.uk/programmes/clinical-imaging/), where I am listed as an author.
eLfH works in partnership with the NHS to support patient care by providing free eLearning sessions to educate
and train the health and social care workforce. eLfH programs are increasingly utilised on staff electronic records
for statutory and mandatory training, enhancing familiarity with the system. With 24/7 access, this platform will
allow clinical staff to view and test their learning at a pace and time that suits them. As part of that, Clinical Imaging
is designed to support imaging staff of all grades and specialist modalities by providing a range of educational
content including image interpretation, patient care, imaging patients with specific needs and clinical governance.
The Clinical Imaging eLearning programme is developed and endorsed by the Society and College of
Radiographers, in collaboration with NHS England.
I was keen to forge this collaboration with eLfH as it will allow every radiographer in the NHS to have free access
to this training program. Additionally, it can be accessed by any healthcare professional in the NHS, or through
OpenAthens, with an interest in implant identification such as nurses, medical students, radiology trainees and
other Allied Health Professional groups.
I am working with a small team to build and implement the programme on the platform:
 Dorothy Keane- Clinical lead of the Clinical Imaging project for the Society and College of Radiographers;
 Dave Beardmore- Senior project manager for NHSE eLearning for healthcare, specialising in Technology
Enhanced Learning (TEL) education;
 Alan Campbell- Learning designer for NHSE eLearning for Healthcare;
 Marco Borri- MRI clinical scientist and MRI safety expert
Additionally, eLfH content is accessible to organisations outside of the NHS for a fee
(https://www.eintegrity.org/healthcare-course/clinical-imaging/). With the right advertising, this will allow the training
program to reach an international audience, the promotion of advanced screening methods globally and increased
revenue for the NHS.
NHS Informatics Merseyside
NHS Informatics Merseyside is an NHS digital technology organisation that ensures technology and innovation are
used to enable better health and care (https://www.imerseyside.nhs.uk/). They will be collaborating with the co-
creation team and myself to create our patient infographic and animation.
Their illustration services will produce two versions of the infographic, to support print and electronic distribution.
Their designers are Adobe Certified Associates in graphic design and illustration and will ensure our co-created
design will engage with the public. Their motion graphic and interactive services will produce our animation. The
illustrated animation will be created in alignment with the campaign content and styling to reinforce the key
messages contained within the infographic to engage patients using an alternative format. The design of both
products will be produced in accordance with the NHS Identity Guidelines and accessibility standards.
Following NHS Identity Guidelines is essential to embed the infographic into local communications, which will likely
require the infographic to be branded by the organisations that choose to use it. This includes features such as
fonts, service branding and tone of voice (e.g., concise, professional and accessible language). Additionally, by
creating an online animation, it must be available for use on a website or mobile device by as many people as
possible (e.g., people with motor difficulties, cognitive impairments/learning disabilities or the hearing impaired).
The development of the infographic will allow adjustments and changes to be made and the animation will be
developed through a storyboard, before finalising the products.
Host Organisation Support Statement
The following statement of support should be composed jointly by the Heads of Department / Senior Managers of
the employing host organisation and the partner organisation.
The Heads of Department / Senior Managers of these organisations will be able to add the statement of support to
the form once they have confirmed participation. Invitations to participate are sent by the applicant via the
'Participants and Signatories' section of the form.
Statement of support
We are delighted to support Hannah’s application for an NIHR Doctoral Clinical and Practitioner Academic
Fellowship (DCAF). Hannah is currently a Senior Magnetic Resonance Imaging Radiographer at The Royal
Marsden NHS Foundation Trust (RM) and the fellowship will be hosted in the RM radiotherapy department in
partnership with The Institute of Cancer Research (ICR) in the Division of Radiotherapy and Imaging. Both
organisations have a long-standing relationship through the NIHR RM/ICR Biomedical Research Centre (BRC)
and are experienced in supporting clinicians such as Hannah to practise during their fellowships, combining
research roles with clinical duties. The supervisory team is led by Professor Helen McNair, a highly experienced
supervisor with 5 completions of MD/PhD and over 14 MSc students. Hannah’s supervisors and wider clinical
team have proudly supported Hannah’s development during her NIHR PCAF and are committed to develop
aspiring clinical academic radiographers during, and after doctoral studies.
The Radiography Department:
Radiography at RM is a recognised centre of clinical excellence combined with successful academic research.
Professor Helen McNair, Hannah’s primary supervisor is herself a HEE/NIHR Senior Clinical Lecturer (2019-2024),
our Trust Lead Research Radiographer. She is Chair of the NIHR Diagnostic and therapeutic Incubator, a
collaboration with Imperial College Academic Health Science Centre. Professor McNair has established a vibrant
community of research-interested and -active radiographers which strives to increase research capacity and
capability across all radiographers. She will be able to play a key role in Hannah’s development as a clinical
academic.
Hannah has been motivated by this positive and supportive research environment to find solutions to a clinical
issue she is particularly interested in, MRI safety. Hannah’s specific clinical expertise in this area, her previous
work in building networks nationally and internationally, and her work on the PCAF makes her an excellent
candidate for an NIHR DCAF. We have no doubt that she will use this fellowship to improve the safe care for all
patients receiving MRIs. The supervisory team includes Dr Christina Malamateniou, Associate Professor in
radiography at City University of London and research lead of the department of radiography. She brings
extensive expertise in Hannahs area of interest, MRI safety but also experience of supervision, using mixed
methods and AI. Hannah will have access to the innovative training Christina offers in AI and other emerging
areas in radiography at City.
The NIHR RM/ICR Biomedical Research Centre (BRC):
The NIHR RM/ICR BRC is the only BRC specifically focused on cancer. The research strategy seeks to inspire
the future generation of researchers and to ensure equality, diversity and inclusion is visible to all. Hannah’s work
aligns with the Imaging and Data Science theme of our BRC as MRI is increasingly used in the cancer diagnosis,
treatment, and staging pathway. The BRC's main priority is to support translational research, and the work Hannah
proposes aligns very strongly with these intentions.
The Institute of Cancer Research
Hannah will be part of the Division of Radiotherapy and Imaging of the ICR, led by Professor Kevin Harrington.
The division includes the prestigious Centre for Cancer Imaging and has long-standing clinical imaging research
programmes in computed tomography (CT) and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). The ICR holds the European
Commission HR Excellence in Research Award and is a signatory of the revised Concordat to support the career
development of researchers and Concordat to support research integrity. ICR has a strong academic supportive
environment and research community, over the 2021/22 academic year, there were 264 people registered on
research degrees with the ICR including 41 new fully funded PhD students. Of ICR non-clinical PhD students
graduating between 2010 and 2019, 91% went directly into research roles.
Training and development for Hannah include courses related to research skills, statistics and scientific computing
and careers and leadership activities that are specifically tailored to researchers at Hannah’s stage, but this will be
complimented with links the supervisors have to professional specific groups and opportunities.
The Institute of Cancer research (ICR), and the RM place equality, diversity, and inclusion (EDI) at the forefront of
their joint research strategy, hosting regular events to promote its importance and monitor actions. Both
organisations attract staff and students from across the world from completely different backgrounds with a variety
of religious, sexual and gender identities. We are proud of this incredible diversity.
The Royal Marsden NHS Foundation Trust:
The RM prides itself on its track record for developing clinical academic careers for Nurses, Allied Health
Professionals and Pharmacists (NAHPPs) and is fully supportive of Hannah’s application. She is already part of
our community of researchers pursuing a clinical academic pathway including staff on internships [n= 1 HEE/NIHR
programme; n=4 internally], pre-doctoral fellowship [n=9 internally funded; n=4 NIHR PCAF] and doctoral
programmes [n=11, 2 on NIHR DCAF] and NIHR SCPRA awards (n=2). All staff on these programmes are
expected to participate in a monthly research meeting. Hannah gets an opportunity to share her work with the
group and learn from staff who are further along the pathway i.e. staff doing an NIHR doctoral fellowship or who
can share their experiences of patient and public engagement as early-career researchers. In addition, we have
20 NAHPPs who are post-doctoral and act as role models throughout the Trust.
Professor Susanne Cruickshank, the Strategic Lead for Applied Health Research, is one of 15 Research Leads in
RM. She has overall responsibility for the clinical academic programmes for NAHPPs within the Trust. As an
experienced researcher, cancer expert and educator she provides leadership and guidance so staff can flourish as
researchers and will continue to act as one of Hannah’s supervisors. She has 6 PhD and over 20 MRES/MSc
completions across different disciplines and brings a breadth of research methodological experience.
Hannah would continue to be supported beyond the completion of this doctoral fellowship to enable her to develop
her future clinical academic career by our organisations. Overall, this collaborative support from The Royal
Marsden, ICR and her supervisory team represents an unparalleled training opportunity for Hannah and
foundation for a leadership role in research.

support_text.text:
This program has been developed using the Vitae researcher development
PhD program
The Institute of Cancer Research (ICR) has a long-standing clinical imaging research programme, including MRI,
and provides training in the skills necessary to excel in research. These range from interpersonal skills and
leadership to performing scientific research and project/resource management. There is an extensive catalogue to
supplement my learning, some of which are included below. Additionally, I can undertake modules from
Perspectives in Oncology. These provide a basic grounding in cancer science including medical physics, structural
biology, and cancer treatment, complementing my clinical career.
Research methods
I completed an introductory course in qualitative research methods and analysis, including content analysis.
However, further learning to report qualitative research will assist this project:
 Reporting qualitative data, NatCen.
Additionally, as I have no experience in mixed-methods research, I need to complete the following:
 Mixed-methods in health research, University of Oxford.
I lack experience in evaluating complex interventions and statistics. I'll complete the following to help with
intervention evaluation:
 Introduction to Statistics and Critical Appraisal/Further Statistics for Health Science Researchers,
University of Sheffield;
 Applied Principles and Practice of Behaviour Change, University College London;
 Developing and Evaluating Complex Interventions, NCRM;
 Intervention design and evaluation, University of Sheffield.
I'll take the following to learn how to visualise data effectively and design infographics, promoting more effective
communication between the co-creation and professional graphics teams:
 Design and make Infographics, Coursera.
This project requires both co-design and co-creation work. Therefore, I will complete the following:
 Experience-based co-design and Facilitation skills course, The Point of Care Foundation;
 Co-design for All: Doing co-design in practice, Coursera.
Completing the experience-based co-design course allows participation in the Point of Care Foundation’s
community of practice, an online space to reflect, share ideas and learn from others working in co-design.
I'll attend a Participatory Action Research (PAR) course tailored to those with basic knowledge of qualitative
research methods. It covers ethical considerations, establishing rapport with participants, and involving
communities in PAR projects:
 Introduction to PAR, SRA.
To enhance my rapport with patients and research participants, I'll attend the following to complement courses
completed during the PCAF:
 The Inclusive Communication Course, Course Beetle
 Dealing with difficult situations through forum theatre, NIHR.
I have completed a scoping review, but to perform a systematic review, I need to learn additional skills e.g., quality
assessments and meta-analysis:
 Conducting a Systematic Review: A Practical Guide, Cardiff University.
To assist the dissemination and wider impact of my research, I will complete:
 Creating prize-winning scientific posters;
 Presenting research at a conference, ICR.
Complementary courses
I will complete these ICR courses in year one to ensure a strong start to the project:
 Project management for PhDs;
 Research integrity;
 Managing your research project;
 Maintaining momentum during a research project;
 Academic writing Part 1&2.
I will then complete the following to build on this foundation knowledge and strengthen my understanding of
governance, ethics and project management:
 Project Management and Research Governance short course, University of Birmingham.
Leadership and Education
I will complete these courses to develop my skills as a future research leader and educator, specifically the
development of learning resources, understanding educational theories, the appropriate use of different media and
assessing educational impact:
 Managing successful blended teams/Exploring the imposter syndrome for clinical researchers, ICR;
 Emerging clinical leaders programme, The King’s Fund;
 Educational media and technologies, Cardiff University;
 Assessing learning, Cardiff University.
Additionally, The Royal Marsden (RMH) is partnered with the Imperial College Academic Health Science Centre,
including the Clinical Academic Training Office, where I can access free training, workshops and events to support
my learning and networking throughout the award.
Continuing Professional Development
To maintain and develop my clinical and MRI safety-specific knowledge I will attend the following:
 The MRI Safety Officer course teaches MRI staff how to mitigate risks when scanning patients with
unknown medical implants and foreign bodies in situ.
 MRI safety and innovation conference, brings together MRI safety experts from around the world to
discuss the latest advancements, issues, and innovations in MRI safety.
 UK Imaging and Oncology (UKIO) is an annual, multidisciplinary conference covering all aspects of
diagnostic imaging and radiation oncology, radiology informatics, and service delivery. Attendance will be
to enhance my clinical knowledge, participate in research hubs (especially in year two where I hope to
recruit radiographers for my intervention evaluation), and present my work, if appropriate.
 The Introduction to Artificial Intelligence for radiographers course will introduce how AI is applied in
radiography contexts. It explores its strengths/weaknesses, and its impact on practice, workflows and the
patient pathway. Completion will aid in planning my proposed post-doctoral work, City University.
Clinical skills
My primary clinical role will remain at RMH, a specialist oncology and tertiary referral centre, that exposes me to
patients with complex medical histories and MRI screening challenges. With support from managers and
supervisors within the Trust, I’ll position myself as an MRI screening specialist, tailoring teaching and training to
colleagues and the wider MRI team. Additionally, RMH is building capacity for clinical academic radiographers and
applied health research. With guidance and support from my academic/practice supervisors and managers, I aim
to transition into a clinical academic role upon completing the award.
I’ll continue participating in radiology’s journal clubs, and Southwest London Imaging Academy training. I’ll
participate in RMH’s monthly research and audit forums and attend the Trust’s annual research day to foster and
maintain research networks within my organisation.
To complement my experience, I'll organise secondments to settings that face screening challenges, provide
scanning of complex implants and areas where I have no clinical experience:
 Mobile MRI/Community Diagnostic Centres where infrastructure for viewing medical records may be
limited, and support from radiologists/medical physicists may be reduced;
 A specialist cardiac centre, where I can familiarise myself with cardiac MRI, one of the UK’s largest
expansions of all imaging modalities, particularly for imaging patients with non-conditional (untested in
MRI) pacemakers;
 7Tesla (Ultra-high field strength) MRI where independent safety testing of implants/devices is crucial due
to the higher field strength not typically encountered in clinical practice.

Name Professor Helen McNair
Professor of Translational Therapeutic Radiographer and Lead Clinical
Institution
Academic Radiographer

Description of support provided
Professor McNair is fully committed to supervising Hannah to complete a robust PhD. She has >100 publications
and is a frequent invited speaker, not only regarding her research areas but also in developing clinical academic
roles. She has supervised 14 MSc students, 2 MD’s and 3 PhD's. She is currently supervising one PhD, one pre-
application NIHR award and one professional doctorate project. She commits to meeting with Hannah once a
fortnight.
Previously a holder of a HEEE/NIHR senior clinical lectureship, currently mentoring two NIHR doctoral candidates
and PI of a NIHR incubator for diagnostic and therapeutic radiographers, she will be able to apply her extensive
experience in creating clinical academic pathways to advise Hannah.
Her research interests include developing training, assessing competency, and implementing new workflows. As a
therapeutic radiographer working on a MR-Linac she is acutely aware of the MRI safety screening issues. Via
training in her NIHR grant she has improved her skills in qualitative research to enable her to co-supervise all
aspects of the research. She has supervised Hannah's predoctoral work and looks forward to continuing to be part
of Hannah’s journey in becoming a clinical academic and effective researcher.
As a ICR team leader, Helen will use her links to ensure Hannah has access to relevant facilities and training. She
works closely with the clinical academic training office at Imperial College , liaises with the Biomedical Research
Centre, a partnership between RM and ICR and works alongside Professor Susanne Cruickshank, Applied Health
Research lead at RM.

several projects related to MRI safety, and I supported Hannah in presenting them at national (IPEM) and
international (ISMRM) leading conferences on MRI safety and to publish a scientific article in Radiography. This
also led to our ongoing collaboration with eLearning for Healthcare (NHS England).
I have been a clinical and academic supervisor, and mentor, for 12 MSc students, including Hannah. As a Clinical
Scientist and former NIHR doctoral fellow, I am in a strong position to advise and support Hannah on the wider
aspects of both her professional development and her fellowship project. An integral part of my current role is to
empower radiographers to broaden their horizons and develop their clinical role, by training them and providing
them with professional and scientific support. As a certified MR Safety Expert and member of national and
international boards on MRI Safety, I am also best placed to advise Hannah on the wider reach of her project,
making sure that her fellowship and career fit into the broader conversation on MRI Safety. I have discussed these
aspects with Hannah in preparation for her application, and I plan to meet with her for an hour on a bi-monthly
basis during the fellowship to support her in her clinical and professional development.
PRACTICE SUPERVISOR 3
Name Erica Scurr DCR (R) MSc MRSO (MBMS TM)
Institution The Royal Marsden NHS Foundation Trust
Position Cross-Site Lead MRI Superintendent Radiographer
Description of support provided
I am the MRI lead radiographer responsible for clinical MRI and research services at the Royal Marsden. I am a
clinical specialist having worked in MRI for 32 years and specialising in oncology for over 20 years. I am a
respected professional, leader and educator in this field.
Since Hannah joined the Trust, I have encouraged her to develop her work as I believe it has a pivotal role in the
education of patients and staff, highlighting the importance of safe practices in MRI. With the rapid growth in MRI
services, it is important to implement accessible learning to support our workforce in all aspects of MRI safety.
Implanted devices present a particular challenge, especially in compromised and vulnerable patients. Recognising
implants before MRI is critical to positive patient outcomes. This has been known since MRI's clinical inception, but
Hannah has had the vision and drive to formalise implant identification which will be welcomed by the MRI
community and could have national and global reach.
I am passionate about patient safety and the benefit that the e-learning module will have in supporting staff to
ensure safe, high-quality patient-centred care. The infographic will inform patients and empower them to work with
radiographic professionals, ensuring safety during their imaging pathway.
In my clinical lead role, I will be available to work with Hannah to ensure that she receives the support required to
meet the aims of this valuable project and provide mentorship as she continues to develop her leadership and
academic career.
Collaborations
The Society and College of Radiographers (SCoR) and eLearning for Healthcare (eLfH, NHS England).
My eLearning training intervention will be held in the Clinical Imaging section of the eLfH platform (https://www.e-
lfh.org.uk/programmes/clinical-imaging/), where I am listed as an author.
eLfH works in partnership with the NHS to support patient care by providing free eLearning sessions to educate
and train the health and social care workforce. eLfH programs are increasingly utilised on staff electronic records
for statutory and mandatory training, enhancing familiarity with the system. With 24/7 access, this platform will
allow clinical staff to view and test their learning at a pace and time that suits them. As part of that, Clinical Imaging
is designed to support imaging staff of all grades and specialist modalities by providing a range of educational
content including image interpretation, patient care, imaging patients with specific needs and clinical governance.
The Clinical Imaging eLearning programme is developed and endorsed by the Society and College of
Radiographers, in collaboration with NHS England.
I was keen to forge this collaboration with eLfH as it will allow every radiographer in the NHS to have free access
to this training program. Additionally, it can be accessed by any healthcare professional in the NHS, or through
OpenAthens, with an interest in implant identification such as nurses, medical students, radiology trainees and
other Allied Health Professional groups.
I am working with a small team to build and implement the programme on the platform:
 Dorothy Keane- Clinical lead of the Clinical Imaging project for the Society and College of Radiographers;
 Dave Beardmore- Senior project manager for NHSE eLearning for healthcare, specialising in Technology
Enhanced Learning (TEL) education;
 Alan Campbell- Learning designer for NHSE eLearning for Healthcare;
 Marco Borri- MRI clinical scientist and MRI safety expert
Additionally, eLfH content is accessible to organisations outside of the NHS for a fee
(https://www.eintegrity.org/healthcare-course/clinical-imaging/). With the right advertising, this will allow the training
program to reach an international audience, the promotion of advanced screening methods globally and increased
revenue for the NHS.
NHS Informatics Merseyside
NHS Informatics Merseyside is an NHS digital technology organisation that ensures technology and innovation are
used to enable better health and care (https://www.imerseyside.nhs.uk/). They will be collaborating with the co-
creation team and myself to create our patient infographic and animation.
Their illustration services will produce two versions of the infographic, to support print and electronic distribution.
Their designers are Adobe Certified Associates in graphic design and illustration and will ensure our co-created
design will engage with the public. Their motion graphic and interactive services will produce our animation. The
illustrated animation will be created in alignment with the campaign content and styling to reinforce the key
messages contained within the infographic to engage patients using an alternative format. The design of both
products will be produced in accordance with the NHS Identity Guidelines and accessibility standards.
Following NHS Identity Guidelines is essential to embed the infographic into local communications, which will likely
require the infographic to be branded by the organisations that choose to use it. This includes features such as
fonts, service branding and tone of voice (e.g., concise, professional and accessible language). Additionally, by
creating an online animation, it must be available for use on a website or mobile device by as many people as
possible (e.g., people with motor difficulties, cognitive impairments/learning disabilities or the hearing impaired).
The development of the infographic will allow adjustments and changes to be made and the animation will be
developed through a storyboard, before finalising the products.
Host Organisation Support Statement
The following statement of support should be composed jointly by the Heads of Department / Senior Managers of
the employing host organisation and the partner organisation.
The Heads of Department / Senior Managers of these organisations will be able to add the statement of support to
the form once they have confirmed participation. Invitations to participate are sent by the applicant via the
'Participants and Signatories' section of the form.
Statement of support
We are delighted to support Hannah’s application for an NIHR Doctoral Clinical and Practitioner Academic
Fellowship (DCAF). Hannah is currently a Senior Magnetic Resonance Imaging Radiographer at The Royal
Marsden NHS Foundation Trust (RM) and the fellowship will be hosted in the RM radiotherapy department in
partnership with The Institute of Cancer Research (ICR) in the Division of Radiotherapy and Imaging. Both
organisations have a long-standing relationship through the NIHR RM/ICR Biomedical Research Centre (BRC)
and are experienced in supporting clinicians such as Hannah to practise during their fellowships, combining
research roles with clinical duties. The supervisory team is led by Professor Helen McNair, a highly experienced
supervisor with 5 completions of MD/PhD and over 14 MSc students. Hannah’s supervisors and wider clinical
team have proudly supported Hannah’s development during her NIHR PCAF and are committed to develop
aspiring clinical academic radiographers during, and after doctoral studies.
The Radiography Department:
Radiography at RM is a recognised centre of clinical excellence combined with successful academic research.
Professor Helen McNair, Hannah’s primary supervisor is herself a HEE/NIHR Senior Clinical Lecturer (2019-2024),
our Trust Lead Research Radiographer. She is Chair of the NIHR Diagnostic and therapeutic Incubator, a
collaboration with Imperial College Academic Health Science Centre. Professor McNair has established a vibrant
community of research-interested and -active radiographers which strives to increase research capacity and
capability across all radiographers. She will be able to play a key role in Hannah’s development as a clinical
academic.
Hannah has been motivated by this positive and supportive research environment to find solutions to a clinical
issue she is particularly interested in, MRI safety. Hannah’s specific clinical expertise in this area, her previous
work in building networks nationally and internationally, and her work on the PCAF makes her an excellent
candidate for an NIHR DCAF. We have no doubt that she will use this fellowship to improve the safe care for all
patients receiving MRIs. The supervisory team includes Dr Christina Malamateniou, Associate Professor in
radiography at City University of London and research lead of the department of radiography. She brings
extensive expertise in Hannahs area of interest, MRI safety but also experience of supervision, using mixed
methods and AI. Hannah will have access to the innovative training Christina offers in AI and other emerging
areas in radiography at City.
The NIHR RM/ICR Biomedical Research Centre (BRC):
The NIHR RM/ICR BRC is the only BRC specifically focused on cancer. The research strategy seeks to inspire
the future generation of researchers and to ensure equality, diversity and inclusion is visible to all. Hannah’s work
aligns with the Imaging and Data Science theme of our BRC as MRI is increasingly used in the cancer diagnosis,
treatment, and staging pathway. The BRC's main priority is to support translational research, and the work Hannah
proposes aligns very strongly with these intentions.
The Institute of Cancer Research
Hannah will be part of the Division of Radiotherapy and Imaging of the ICR, led by Professor Kevin Harrington.
The division includes the prestigious Centre for Cancer Imaging and has long-standing clinical imaging research
programmes in computed tomography (CT) and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). The ICR holds the European
Commission HR Excellence in Research Award and is a signatory of the revised Concordat to support the career
development of researchers and Concordat to support research integrity. ICR has a strong academic supportive
environment and research community, over the 2021/22 academic year, there were 264 people registered on
research degrees with the ICR including 41 new fully funded PhD students. Of ICR non-clinical PhD students
graduating between 2010 and 2019, 91% went directly into research roles.
Training and development for Hannah include courses related to research skills, statistics and scientific computing
and careers and leadership activities that are specifically tailored to researchers at Hannah’s stage, but this will be
complimented with links the supervisors have to professional specific groups and opportunities.
The Institute of Cancer research (ICR), and the RM place equality, diversity, and inclusion (EDI) at the forefront of
their joint research strategy, hosting regular events to promote its importance and monitor actions. Both
organisations attract staff and students from across the world from completely different backgrounds with a variety
of religious, sexual and gender identities. We are proud of this incredible diversity.
The Royal Marsden NHS Foundation Trust:
The RM prides itself on its track record for developing clinical academic careers for Nurses, Allied Health
Professionals and Pharmacists (NAHPPs) and is fully supportive of Hannah’s application. She is already part of
our community of researchers pursuing a clinical academic pathway including staff on internships [n= 1 HEE/NIHR
programme; n=4 internally], pre-doctoral fellowship [n=9 internally funded; n=4 NIHR PCAF] and doctoral
programmes [n=11, 2 on NIHR DCAF] and NIHR SCPRA awards (n=2). All staff on these programmes are
expected to participate in a monthly research meeting. Hannah gets an opportunity to share her work with the
group and learn from staff who are further along the pathway i.e. staff doing an NIHR doctoral fellowship or who
can share their experiences of patient and public engagement as early-career researchers. In addition, we have
20 NAHPPs who are post-doctoral and act as role models throughout the Trust.
Professor Susanne Cruickshank, the Strategic Lead for Applied Health Research, is one of 15 Research Leads in
RM. She has overall responsibility for the clinical academic programmes for NAHPPs within the Trust. As an
experienced researcher, cancer expert and educator she provides leadership and guidance so staff can flourish as
researchers and will continue to act as one of Hannah’s supervisors. She has 6 PhD and over 20 MRES/MSc
completions across different disciplines and brings a breadth of research methodological experience.
Hannah would continue to be supported beyond the completion of this doctoral fellowship to enable her to develop
her future clinical academic career by our organisations. Overall, this collaborative support from The Royal
Marsden, ICR and her supervisory team represents an unparalleled training opportunity for Hannah and
foundation for a leadership role in research.

(https://patients-voice.cancerbrc.org/the-pre-mri-screening-process-what-are-your-thoughts)
Patient feedback revealed common concerns about the MRI screening process, including:
 Insufficient pre-scan information, particularly about the screening process;
 Misunderstandings about the necessity of declaring medically implanted devices, even those implanted
since childhood;
 Current pre-scan information is often overlooked/not read by patients;
 Pictures and animations could support MRI screening.
PPI Group
The group met four times and they influenced the following:
Research plan:
 Incorporating feedback from the Cancer Patients’ Voice platform, the PPI group contributed to the
development of Package 4, the co-creation of an infographic educating patients regarding MRI screening.
They also advised on including an animation accompaniment (with subtitles), to better assist some patient
groups.
Research design:
 They reviewed and agreed on the timelines for each package;
 The PPI group reviewed the study design for Package 4 and endorsed the recommendations. They
suggested recording and summarising meetings for those with memory issues/mental fatigue. Additionally,
they proposed limiting online meetings to 2 hours if conducted virtually;
 The meetings should be outside of a hospital and easily accessible by public transport;
 Recruitment should be from different local Trusts and community spaces to increase diversity;
 They advised forming two PPI groups for the proposed project, as it was considered too much for one
group to advise on the research and co-create the infographic/animation. However, the project PPI group
should be invited to co-facilitate the co-creation meetings. Due to the desired diversity of the co-creation
group, a co-facilitator will help ensure all co-creation members are fully supported (e.g., additional verbal
explanations and writing assistance) and that their opinions are equally represented;
 Dissemination of the infographic via patient forums, social media, and charity affiliations;
 Review and editing the plain English summary
Professional steering group
This group met once during the PCAF. Their main influence was the promotion and dissemination of the eLearning
and infographic interventions. In addition to the UK’s radiography professional body, promotion through UK-based
MRI organisations (the British Association of MRI Radiographers (BAMRR), MRI Safety Matters, Institute of
Medical Physics (IPEM) and ‘Everything MRI’) or UK radiography-focused podcasts (Rad_Chat, Imagin’That) were
suggested.
the proposed research, including any training and support provided
PPI members will receive reimbursement per NIHR payment guidance51. The host organisation will provide
members with introductory training, including understanding PPI and collaborative strategies. Meetings will be
audio recorded and reported using GRIPP2-SF/GRIPP2-LF for PPI and co-creation activities in any published
literature52.
Project PPI
I'll seek up to eight members. We will meet four times in year one to set up the study and organise recruitment
materials, then thrice-yearly afterwards. Involvement will include:
 Providing the patient/public perspective on my study and how I can improve/develop it;
 Designing/reviewing all consents, advertisements and participant information for radiographer, co-creation
and focus group recruitment;
 Designing/reviewing pre-co-creation focus group questions, ensuring appropriateness and
understandability;
 Designing the co-creation project;
 Co-facilitating co-creation meetings;
 Review the final infographic and animation.
Training: As this group will review multiple elements, I will offer ‘Training for reviewing’
(https://www.learningforinvolvement.org.uk/an-interactive-course-for-new-and-experienced-patient-public-
reviewers-of-health-and-social-care-research/). Additionally, as members will be invited to co-facilitate the co-
creation meetings, training in facilitation skills will be offered. Some co-creation members may require more
assistance/attention to ensure their views are heard and expressed to the group.
Co-creation group
This group will meet up to ten times in-person while developing the infographic. Involvement will include:
 Creating the infographic and animation;
 Collaboratively publish an article on the co-creation process.
Training: For those interested, I will direct group members to the NIHR’s co-production webpages,
(https://www.learningforinvolvement.org.uk/topic/co-production/). Additionally, I will offer Co-creation training
delivered by Arts and Homelessness International. The training can be tailored to our project and the group's
needs, especially important considering our group's anticipated diversity.
Both PPI groups will assist disseminating the infographic through charities, community spaces, social media and
patient forums.
Cancer Patients’ Voice
I will post polls, surveys, and ideas on my page throughout the award to gather informal patient feedback.
Professional steering group
We (managers, educators, safety experts) will meet online at least five times. Involvement will include:
 Discussing eLearning pilot results to inform next steps;
 Advising on the content and NHS integration of the infographic, ensuring all Trusts can incorporate it into
their documentation;
 Designing/reviewing eLearning evaluation questions and recruitment assistance;
 Promoting the eLearning training and infographic/animation to the MRI community.
